title: The Paradigm Shift
subtitle: Before instruction can change, understanding must change.
summary: Examines what phonics-first frameworks assume about English spelling and what those assumptions cost students. Demonstrates that systematic student errors are logical products of an inaccurate explanatory framework, not evidence of student failure. Establishes the three layers of the writing system, the meaning-first investigative sequence, and the research base for morphological instruction.
learning_outcomes: Recognize student spelling errors (e.g. *jumpt, *stopt, *wosht) as logical products of phonics-first instruction, not carelessness or deficits
Explain why the suffix <-ed> is morphologically stable across its three pronunciation environments (/t/, /d/, /ɪd/)
Identify false analyses produced by phonics-first instruction, including the invented suffixes <-tion> and <-sion>
Explain why syllabication (e.g. VCCV) destroys morpheme boundaries and obscures meaning
Distinguish the three layers of the writing system: morphology (morpheme), orthography (grapheme), phonology (phoneme)
Apply the meaning-first investigative sequence: Meaning → Structure → Word family → Grapheme function
Contrast phonics-first responses that close investigation with meaning-first responses that open it
Articulate the research finding that morphological instruction most benefits less able and younger students
key_concepts: Morphological stability: morphemes hold their spelling across pronunciation shifts
Grapheme-phoneme correspondences are always contextual — analyzed within a specific word, never in isolation
False suffixes: <-tion> and <-sion> do not exist; the suffix is <-ion>
Syllabication severs morpheme boundaries because it detects sound boundaries, not meaning boundaries
The investigative sequence: Meaning → Structure → Word family → Grapheme function
No irregular spellings — only spellings a sound-first model cannot explain
No silent letters — every grapheme has a function; the question is always: what is it?
Phonics-first characterizations (irregular, silent, exception) are labels that close inquiry
Meaning-first responses open investigation by directing attention to the base and word family
swi_connections: Word sums for <-ed> across phonological environments (jump+ed, jog+ed, land+ed)
Morphological analysis of act+ion and tense/+ion revealing the false <-tion>/<-sion> analysis
The investigative sequence as the foundation for all SWI inquiry
int_connections: Assumed competence: student spelling errors (e.g. *jumpt, *stopt) reflect the limits of the instructional framework, not the limits of the student — reframing error as evidence of logical reasoning within a flawed system

Deficit language has cognitive and affective costs: framing students as careless, non-retaining, or memory-impaired increases demand-sensitivity and erodes self-concept; the paradigm shift in Module 1 is an affect-regulation intervention as much as a cognitive one

Morphological stability provides predictability: a system in which morphemes hold their spelling is cognitively stabilizing; arbitrary exceptions signal system unreliability, which is dysregulating for students with language-based learning differences

Generative tools reduce cognitive load: one morphological principle (morphemes hold their spelling across pronunciation shifts) accounts for a large class of words that phonics-first instruction requires students to memorize individually — the load reduction scales with the generativity of the principle

Learner agency through transferable inquiry: the meaning-first investigative sequence gives students a repeatable tool they can apply independently across new words — reducing dependence on teacher explanation and increasing autonomous engagement with the writing system
Principled understanding replaces arbitrary rule-exception learning, eliminating the working memory demand of storing a growing list of exceptions that carry no generative value
format: Reading · comprehension checks · formal assessment · written reflection

sections.title: A student who did everything right
sections.summary: The *jumpt* case: why phonetically logical but orthographically inaccurate spellings reveal a framework problem, not a student problem. Morphological stability of the <-ed> suffix.

sections.title: What phonics-first instruction produces
sections.summary: How positioning sound as the primary explanatory system obscures meaning. Graphemes analyzed in context. Comparison table: does, sign/signal, love, sugar, knowledge.

sections.title: When the framework invents its own evidence
sections.summary: The false suffixes <-tion> and <-sion>: how phonics-first analysis severs transparent morphological relationships. The real suffix is <-ion>.

sections.title: When syllabication destroys the base
sections.summary: VCCV syllabication (jum·ping) vs. morphological analysis (jump+ing). Why syllable divisions have no mechanism for detecting morpheme boundaries.

sections.title: Three systems, one writing system
sections.summary: Morphology, orthography, and phonology as distinct layers. The principled hierarchy: morphology governs spelling; phonology describes pronunciation but does not determine it.

sections.title: A practitioner's moment of recognition
sections.summary: Founder's reflection on the shift from phonics-first to meaning-first. Research grounding: Bowers & Kirby (2010), Goodwin & Ahn (2010, 2013) on morphological instruction.

sections.title: What changes in the classroom
sections.summary: Side-by-side comparison of phonics-first and meaning-first teacher responses to five common classroom situations. Meaning-first responses are more precise, not softer.

gate_criteria: Student error is described as logical within a phonics-first frame — not as careless, unmotivated, or evidence of a learning deficit
Word investigation moves from meaning and structure before addressing sound or grapheme function
The distinction between rule-memorization and principled understanding is articulated in concrete, instructional terms
No phonics-first characterizations (silent letter, irregular, exception) are used without being named and examined as such
references: Bowers, P. N., & Kirby, J. R. (2010). Effects of morphological instruction on vocabulary acquisition. Reading and Writing, 23, 515–537.

Bowers, P. N., Kirby, J. R., & Deacon, S. H. (2010). The effects of morphological instruction on literacy skills: A systematic review of the literature. Review of Educational Research, 80(2), 144–179.
Goodwin, A. P., & Ahn, S. (2010). A meta-analysis of morphological interventions: Effects on literacy achievement of children with literacy difficulties. Annals of Dyslexia, 60, 183–208.
Goodwin, A. P., & Ahn, S. (2013). A meta-analysis of morphological interventions in English: Effects on literacy outcomes for school-age children. Scientific Studies of Reading, 17(4), 257–285.
Venezky, R. L. (1999). The American way of spelling: The structure and origins of American English orthography. Guilford Press.
Bowers, P. N. (2022). Structured Word Inquiry (SWI): Literacy instruction that makes sense of English spelling for students of all ages and abilities. PsyArXiv. https://doi.org/10.31234/osf.io/aktzw
